author: @michblitz

author: @rbdogwood
text: A very informative development by trial experiment. My objective would be to have wind driven electric generators (and solar panels) on every roof. In Scotland (central belt) we have a fair bit of wind, I can see several dozen turbines from the top of my house, but I'd like to do something myself. I just need to combine cheap construction, durability, safety, visual unobtrusiveness, silent running and aesthetic appearance. Obviously I cannot have 100% of all of those, but If I value those in that order what basic style would you start with? Excellent stuff by the way.

author: @_John_Sean_Walker
text:
Happy New Year!
This one has great looks!
I only hope you are willing to give it a thought to place the generator close to the central axis so it would be more simple and modular.
It also spins more easy with little wind, and builds up a good resistance at higher wind speed.
It looks promising for poor areas.

author: @lylehintz7275
text:
For a low wind speed, how about using two on the same shaft with one rotating clockwise with magnets on the bottom and another below it rotating counter clockwise with coils on the top.
This seems like you could get twice the rotational difference between the magnets and the coils at the same wind speed.

Might need some type of commutator to transfer to electrical power of the rotating coils.

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering 
I always thought the wind of a passing train was a source waiting to be harvested. But let's say you had a bunch of harvesters lining the track.  Would that put a significant amount of drag on the train, causing the train to use more energy to get up to speed?  Or would it cause a sort of draft (like in cycling) that would actually make it easier for the train reach speed? Or, to be complete, no difference at all.

author: @ScarletFlames1
text:
Well, they would be right, it probably CAN'T handle high speeds.

Just like any other thing that moves, it can't ever handle more than it was made to handle, that's why they install brakes on cars, or on wind turbines, or generators.. I think you get it by now that if something gets spun by outside forces, you can bet your ass someone installed a brake on it.
Shouldn't be too difficult putting a speed sensor (or even simply a mechanical rotation "counter") and have it trigger a piston with a brake pad on it. Maybe have it function like a potentiometer so that the faster it goes the harder it'll push the brake pad thus the more resistance against high wind tearing the thing apart.

Of course if something comes in that could literally tear it off the roof, there's not much you can do to defend against that anyway.

I'm "The glass half-empty, how can I fill it up?" kind of guy.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: well - it's about seeing answers rather than problems - I mean - we actually don't know if it needs brakes or not. You are assuming it does and then creating a problem which needs a solution and presenting solutions - which is more than most do - most stop at the problem and let that stop them - anyway I ramble - the VAWT is supposed to be self limiting - I am not sure if it is or at what speed it is - but I would test it to see if it needs brakes or not first or if it just limits itself

author: @TJBRENTON
text:
Great stuff!  
Wonder what effect having an open side profile has.  is most of the 'off air' now escaping down out the bottom?  Did someone say smokebomb?

I cant help but think shrouding might make a huge difference to these drums.  Weather cocking around the central axis, with a suitable drag source to keep it pointing  in to wind?  The ducting, especially if well done could increase air speed hitting the rotor quite considerably, and mask the forward going side of the drum. A shroud could also be used to regulate speed when electrical load has reached its maximum.

Shame about the axial wobble.  Might make minimising the magnet/coil separation a  bit tricky.

The days of experimentation here are almost limitless....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @kuhrd
text: While you will likely get a few watts out of this, it will never deliver anywhere close to the power of a small solar panel of the same area or even a small 3 or 5 blade wind turbine.  If you have a bunch of this junk lying around, I guess there is potential but VAWT are well researched and we know the disadvantages well, but seems like a lot of effort for very little useful reward.

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text:
This is good to open peoples eyes to see that there are things all around us that can be used to make things. As for a glass being half full or half empty, I believe an engineer would not use either term, an engineer would say that the glass is about twice as large as it needs to be.
While on the subject of reusing materials I’ve become aware of a terrible waist problem. The problem is the bladder out of wine casks, in my part of the world people buy filtered water that comes in them and I don’t think they get recycled. I was thinking of making an inflatable car cover to protect cars from hail or even making rain coats. I believe this type of plastic is welded using dielectric welding so maybe a good project for you might be  to make a dielectric welder to demonstrate how to re use some this material.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a good project mate - I will look into it

replies.author: @Cooliemasteroz
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I’II be looking out to see if you come up with something. When you pull one out of the box you’ll see that it can be spread out like a flat envelope and I’m kicking myself for not mentioning this a while back when you were making bellows for a home air conditioner as it might have been a tidy option to use.
Also a bit of a far out idea I had when I was trying to help a cattle farmer out here in Australia who was effected by the drought that was not too severe in her part of the country. It started with another idea to experiment with  a black parabolic reflector to reflect ultra violet light at solar panels kept in the shape to keep them cool to see if it was worthwhile. I noticed at my house that grass grows better in the shade and I assumed that it was because the soil didn’t dry out as much in these areas and the lack of sunlight was not a problem as it reflects around, particularly the ultra violet. Then while at the same time communicating with this farmer over the internet I thought of getting hundreds of these wine cask bladders and glueing them inflated to large sheets of shade cloth, dragging them out to a paddock were the cattle have moved on from and leaving it there for a while. Not only would it reduce drying out of the soil but it might actually collect condensed water on the under side of these inflated pillows. Just today on TV there was a documentary that I wasn’t paying too much attention to as I was deep in thought but it was about a beetle in some dry desert somewhere in the world that survives by collecting water condensate on its back and directs it to its mouth. This restored my faith in my idea as the farmer thought It was a bit hair brained. If you think it’s a good idea and you know people pass onto then feel free to spread the word because as a full time carer I’ll never have the time to implement it and I just like to see my ideas work.

author: @paulrichards1061
text:
Hi. Clearly picks up the lower wind speed well so it should provide the basis of a low wind speed start up generator. 
Having played with wind and water turbines for years, it is all about the Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) device that sits between the generator and the power destination to always get the most out of all generation levels as it is never a linear relationship.

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
The nay sayers are fun, aren't they?

I would expect that turbine would hold up at least to speeds a drier drum would experience in its native casing. There's no forces on the blades that would cause them to fail like a comertial wind turbine. The blade speed is uniform across the entire blade.  The bearing would be more likely to fail before the blade, and the bearing dosen't care if it's a drum in a drier or blades in the wind, as long as it's balanced.  That's why people love VAWTs

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text: note, that a washing machine drum is full of holes....

replies.author: @MrBunkerstudio
replies.text: @TheBaconWizard thanks cool mate, looks like S, Steal. I like the principal from robs video, I  have the sides off I'm pleased with the free concrete block lol I need hundreds more to make a path, I'm planning on using the outside 4 sided outer frame to house the rotor! If possible, then slice the windcatchers and add alloy fin,s if possible! The charging side of my 12v batteries is a fear of mine! But I'm learning but slowly! But I'm happy with the engineering of the video!

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text: @MrBunkerstudio Those concrete blocks are the bane of my life when trying to move the damn things, especially up and down stairs lol. Anyway good luck.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I would love to see your progress mate - any chance of a vid or two?

replies.author: @MrBunkerstudio
replies.text: @TheBaconWizard that will tax your brain, and back!! At times like that, search YouTube for right said Fred , Bernard cribbins , , makes me feel better lol

text:

to there tune mate

replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering You don't get to 180k subscriptions by being wrong mate. I'm proud of you and envious but not jealous and bitter

If we all wished each other well the world would be a better place

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @popuptoaster
text:
I think these drum types have a better chance of surviving high winds than the more usual bladed type as they are somewhat speed limited by their design, possibly more efficient at low wind speeds and more durable at the expense of higher wind speed effectiveness?

 I would still be inclined to have some sort of braking system fitted I think, just in case, I had a rotating top on my solid fuel burner chimney and the bearings did not last long in the high winds last winter, it's warranty replacement didn't do much better and they refused another one when they found out it was on a boat, saying they were not designed to be exposed to wind coming across open water. :S

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes I thought that too - after all the drum type and rotating roof ventilator have a lot in common

author: @johnmcginnis5201
text:
Some people..... This design like the last one can solve the overspeed problem by using the same coils as a brake as it can to generate a charge. 

As it is, this is a great use of materials. Fact better than your first series that built up to a rotor. I might go for heftier bearings than the skateboard style you used. But still its an excellent bodger job Sir.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think you are right on the bearings mate and if I come across some I will swap them out

author: @kreynolds1123
text: 1 revolution in 6 seconds, 10 rpm. I'm thinking, wow what a challenge to build a generator to get reasonable voltage, but that was very low wind. But then i remembered, V=Q/C  voltage=charge/capacitance.  A capacitive based generator with one set of plates charged to a high voltage with respect to ground can induce large voltage swings in the other plate connected to ground through a very very very light load.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like that mate - mostly because I love capacitors and we did do that electron pump thing in a previous video!
